data.title: Bank of Canada Neutral Hold (2.25%) - USD/CAD Rallies To 1.3
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The Bank of Canada just released its policy rate decision, maintaining rates unchanged for the fourth time since October 2025 and, quite frankly, not hinting at much change in its stance.

The statement had nothing particularly surprising, with the Bank noting that the outlook isn't much different from that indicated in the January Decision.

Some concerns about the quarterly MPC projections regarding the economic outlook maintain the bank's view of a not-so-strong Canadian economy, which takes some pricing out of rate hikes.

Nonetheless, the BoC assumed a $75 crude oil barrel, so if it stays closer to $100 for the next meeting, the Bank should turn more hawkish.

On the loonie, it yoyo'd quite aggressively throughout the ups and downs of the Middle Eastern war – With WTI crude bouncing back above $100 just today, the CAD is seeing a two-catalyst recipe for its daily performance; at least against other major currencies (with USD traders awaiting the FOMC).

Even if the war really settles, the Canadian dollar should not regain its prior lows, with increased oil revenues and orders, which would underpin the CAD for the next few months at least – the BoC mentioned this in relation to oil developments.

"While the war in Iran may alter its composition, overall GDP growth is little changed in the updated forecast: Since Canada is a large net exporter of oil, higher oil prices increase national income even as consumers are squeezed by higher gasoline prices."

Let's dive right into a two-timeframe USD/CAD analysis.

USD/CAD Daily and Intraday Technical Analysis

USD/CAD Daily Chart

USD/CAD has officially stalled its correction, now bouncing from its 1.3660 support zone.

With the BoC not showing many hawkish signs, the CAD is immediately losing some strength, and this should normally extend the price action back towards the 50-Day Moving Average (1.37330).

Above 1.3750, expect to see further rallies in the North American pair back towards 1.39.

USD/CAD 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The FX pair has officially broken its downward channel, and having passed above its 50- and 200-hour MAs, the rebound should see continuation.

Look for a break above the 1.3710, particularly if the FOMC adds fuel to the fire in the US dollar – it will be Jerome Powell's ultimate press conference, so don't expect anything too crazy there.

Levels to place on your USD/CAD charts:

Resistance Levels:

  • 1.3720 – 1.3750 Pivot 50-Day Moving Average (1.37330).
  • 1.38 mini-Resistance +/- 150 pips
  • 1.3850 - 1.3870 Momentum Pivot (Channel retest 1.3860)
  • 1.39 to 1.3925 Support turned resistance

Support Levels:

  • 1.3675 200-Hour MA
  • 1.3630 to 1.3660 Key Support
  • 1.3550 Main 2025 Support (Range Lows)
  • End-January Lows 1.34820

data.title: Driehaus Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The first quarter of 2026 saw a resurgence in global market volatility, as conflict in the Middle East drove a sharp rise in energy prices. At the same time, questions around artificial intelligence (AI) disruption of the software industry continued to intensify, while early signs of stress emerged in segments such as private credit.

During the quarter, emerging markets (EM) outperformed the MSCI All-Country World Index by nearly 300 basis points. Within EM, small caps lagged the broader index by approximately 60 basis points.

Performance Review

The Driehaus Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Strategy gained 6.38% in the first quarter of 2026, outperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index by 712 basis points, as the Index returned -0.74%.1 Stock selection was the primary driver of outperformance, with the largest contributions coming from Industrials and Technology, alongside positive results from Energy and Materials. From a country standpoint, Taiwan, India, and Argentina were the largest contributors, while China, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates were the largest detractors.

Strategy Positioning

On a sector basis, the most significant increase during the quarter was in the materials sector, where the strategy added exposure to companies benefiting from tightening supply in critical minerals and improving visibility for uranium demand. Energy exposure also increased, supported by additions in Latin American exploration and production companies where operational execution remained strong and the fundamental backdrop continued to improve.

These increases were funded primarily by reductions in financials and consumer discretionary. In financials, the strategy exited or reduced positions where deteriorating fundamentals, rising execution risk, or less favorable funding dynamics weakened conviction. In consumer discretionary, exposure was trimmed as near-term earnings momentum slowed in selected retail holdings against a more difficult consumer backdrop in several markets.

Industrials remained the strategy's largest active overweight at quarter-end, reflecting a continued focus on power infrastructure, precision manufacturing, and aerospace-related supply chain exposures. The portfolio also remained underweight technology overall, though exposure was selectively increased in areas most directly tied to AI server demand, optical interconnects, and Low Earth Orbit satellites. The strategy continued to hold no exposure to communication services, which benefited relative performance during the quarter.

1 The performance data represents the strategy's composite of emerging markets small cap equity accounts managed by Driehaus Capital Management LLC (DCM) (the composite). Please see the notes section for other important information.

From a country standpoint, Korea saw the largest increase in exposure and moved from an underweight to a slight overweight during the quarter, reflecting new and increased positions in heavy industrial and power equipment companies benefiting from strong global demand for electrical infrastructure. India was reduced and became a deeper underweight, as softer growth expectations and broader risk-off sentiment across Indian equities weighed on near-term momentum. Exposure to China also increased, with China moving to a modest overweight, supported by selective additions in areas such as power grid equipment and other idiosyncratic opportunities. Brazil was reduced, though the strategy maintained its longstanding overweight to the market. The portfolio also continued to hold meaningful off-benchmark exposure to Argentina and Vietnam.

Sector Attribution

Industrials was the largest contributor to relative performance. The strategy's sizable overweight, combined with strong stock selection, drove the result as holdings tied to grid investment, electrical infrastructure, and industrial demand outperformed. Key contributors included a Korean heavy electrical equipment manufacturer, a Taiwanese precision components supplier, and an Indian power transmission company, all of which benefited from an accelerating capital spending cycle tied to electricity demand, grid modernization, and AI-related power needs.

Technology was the next largest contributor on a relative basis despite the strategy's underweight to the sector. Strong stock selection more than offset the allocation headwind, led by Taiwanese holdings tied to silicon photonics, server management semiconductors, and radio-frequency and waveguide components exposed to Low Earth Orbit satellite demand. These positions benefited from continued strength in AI infrastructure spending and improving visibility across select areas of the small cap technology supply chain.

Energy also contributed meaningfully to relative performance. Results were supported by both the strategy's overweight and favorable stock selection, led by an Argentine shale oil producer that benefited from strong operational execution, lower costs, and a more supportive upstream policy backdrop. A Brazilian offshore producer and a Greek refining company also contributed positively.

On the detractor side, consumer staples was the largest sector drag on relative performance, driven primarily by stock selection. An Indian spirits company was the largest detractor within the sector, as shares reversed following earnings amid concerns around potential excise tax changes, management turnover, and a broader pullback in richly valued Indian consumer names. A South African health and beauty retailer also weighed on results.

Health care was another source of relative weakness. Indian hospital operators were the primary detractors, as macro headwinds and broader market weakness in India pressured valuations. Although the strategy's underweight to the sector provided some offset, it was not enough to overcome the negative selection effect.

From a country standpoint, Taiwan was the largest relative contributor, as the strategy's holdings substantially outperformed despite the portfolio remaining underweight the market. Performance was driven primarily by Taiwanese technology positions tied to AI infrastructure and satellite-related demand.

India was the second-largest contributor, as an underweight position combined with holdings that held up better than the benchmark. The strategy's power infrastructure and defense-related exposures in India were important contributors and more than offset weakness in health care and consumer names.

China was the largest country detractor on a relative basis. Although the strategy carried only a modest overweight, holdings underperformed due to negative stock selection. A LiDAR sensor manufacturer was a notable detractor after guiding to weaker near-term revenue and a less favorable product mix, which pressured margin expectations despite healthy unit growth. A Chinese polysilicon producer also weighed on results as prices declined during the quarter amid ongoing overcapacity and regulatory uncertainty.

Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates were also sources of underperformance. In Saudi Arabia, the strategy's underweight was a headwind in a market that held up relatively well, and weak stock selection added to the drag. In the United Arab Emirates, an overweight position combined with disappointing stock selection across infrastructure and aviation-related holdings to weigh on relative performance, with a UAE-listed low-cost carrier among the primary detractors.

Market Outlook

We remain constructive on EM small caps, noting broadening of bottom-up earnings growth across several sectors and geographies, alongside a favorable macro backdrop. A weakening US dollar and positive earnings revisions continue to underpin the asset class.

Within EM equities, the small cap segment remains highly dispersed, creating a compelling opportunity set for active stock selection. Lower levels of sell side analyst coverage and institutional ownership contribute to persistent inefficiencies, which we believe can be exploited through active management.

More broadly, EM small caps remain under-researched and under-owned. This combination of dispersion and inefficiency is increasingly evident in a volatile and rapidly evolving global environment.

Until next month,

Chad Cleaver, Lead Portfolio Manager

Driehaus Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Strategy

Driehaus Capital Management LLC (DCM) is a registered investment adviser with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). DCM provides investment advisory services using growth equity and credit strategies to individuals, organizations, and institutions. The firm consists of all accounts managed by DCM (the Company).

DCM claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®).

COMPOSITE DESCRIPTION

The Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Composite (the Composite) was created in February 2016. An account is considered to be an emerging markets small cap equity account if it seeks to maximize capital appreciation through active investment primarily in a portfolio of emerging market traded equity and equity-convertible securities of smaller capitalization companies with perceived high growth potential.

PERFORMANCE RESULTS

Net of fee returns reflect the payment of advisory fees and in some instances, other fees and expenses such as administrative and custodian fees while the gross of fee returns do not. Please contact our sales, marketing and relationship management department at 312-932-8621.

RISKS

All investments have risks. The strategy invests in foreign securities, including small and mid cap stocks, which may be subject to greater volatility than other investments. During certain periods, the strategy has benefited from unusually strong market conditions. At times, a significant portion of an account's return may be attributable to investments in initial public offerings (IPOs) or concentrations in certain strong performing sectors, such as technology. Returns from IPOs or sector concentrations may not be repeated or consistently achieved in the future. In addition, participating in IPOs and other investments during favorable market conditions may enhance the performance of a strategy with a smaller asset base, and the strategy may not experience similar performance results as its assets grow. Investments in overseas markets can pose more risks than U.S. investments. In addition, the strategy's returns will fluctuate with changes in stock market conditions, currency values, interest rates, foreign government regulations, and economic and political conditions in countries in which the strategy invests. These risks are generally greater when investing in emerging markets.

TERMS

Active share represents the share of portfolio holdings that differ from the benchmark index holdings. Beta is a measure of a portfolio's volatility. A beta of 1.00 implies perfect historical correlation of movement with the market. A higher beta manager will rise and fall more rapidly than the market, whereas a lower beta manager will rise and fall slower. Information Ratio (IR) measures a portfolio manager's ability to generate excess returns relative to a benchmark, but also attempts to identify the consistency of the investor. This ratio will identify if a manager has beaten the benchmark by a lot in a few months or a little every month. The higher the IR the more consistent a manager is and consistency is an ideal trait. R-Squared is a statistical measure that represents the percentage of a fund or security's movements that can be explained by movements in a benchmark index. For fixed-income securities, the benchmark is the T-bill. For equities, the benchmark is the S&P 500. Standard Deviation is a measure of the average deviations of a return series from its mean; often used as a measure of portfolio volatility. A large standard deviation implies that there have been large swings or volatility in the manager's return series. Tracking Error is a divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark. This is often in the context of a hedge or mutual fund that did not work as effectively as intended, creating an unexpected profit or loss instead.

data.title: Lincoln International Begins IPO Process On Impressive Profitability
data.description:
Lincoln International Is Growing And Profitable

Lincoln International, Inc. (LCLN) has filed to raise funds from an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company provides a full range of investment banking advisory services for companies in the U.S. and internationally.

LCLN is producing organic and M&A-driven growth. Profitability and free cash flow have grown impressively.

I expect the IPO will have significant institutional demand.

What Does Lincoln International Do?

Lincoln provides primarily two types of business services: investment banking advisory and valuations & opinions.

These services are typically in the context of a company either raising additional capital or acquiring or being acquired by another company.

Most of LCLN’s revenue comes from investment banking advisory services in the Americas region, but the company provides its services in Europe and the Asia Pacific region.

The firm’s revenue contribution by segment and region for 2025 is depicted in the pie charts below:

The company is currently organized as a partnership, Lincoln International, LP [LILP], which will be divided between controlling partners and non-controlling partners.

Non-controlling partners will have Class B stock, which will be entitled to one vote per share, similar to Class A stock being offered to the public.

Controlling partners will have Class C stock, which will have twenty (20) votes per share and will control the company immediately after the IPO.

Lincoln is led by Chief Executive Officer Robert T. Brown, who has been with the firm for 28 years and has worked at Price Waterhouse.

The company has recorded fair market value investment of $438.6 million as of the end of 2025.

What is Lincoln’s Market?

Lincoln’s primary market is the US investment banking advisory market, which was an estimated $55 billion in 2025, according to a 2026 market research report by Mordor Intelligence.

The market is expected to reach $67.5 billion by 2031, which would represent a CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of 3.55% from 2026 to 2031, a relatively modest expected growth rate.

The industry is forecast to grow from continued sponsor-led M&A activity, a moderate IPO calendar and “expanding fee pools tied to energy-transition financing supported by federal incentives.”

Also, higher-for-longer interest rates, private credit market growth and tougher Basel III requirements may challenge profitability models of certain companies within the industry.

M&A deals provided nearly 41% of deal volume in the U.S. investment banking market in 2025, as the pie chart shows here:

Lincoln faces competition from direct competitors, bulge-bracket banks, private placement advisors, internal valuation teams and middle-market advisory platforms.

Some of its direct competitor peers include:

  • Raymond James (RJF)

  • Evercore (EVR)

  • Lazard (LAZ)

  • Houlihan Lokey (HLI)

  • Jefferies (JEF)

  • Moelis & Company (MC)

  • PJT Partners (PJT)

  • Baird

  • William Blair

  • Others

Recent industry trends have favored firms that are integrating M&A, private credit and equity capital markets activities into a single platform, while companies with extensive enterprise relationships with have material advantages over competitors.

LCLN’s strengths include its PE sponsor coverage, global operating footprint, cross-border M&A expertise

Its opportunities are its ability to cross-sell various complementary services, expansion into financial services via its recent MarshBerry acquisition, and potential for independent advisor gains against large-institution conflict risks.

Threats to its growth prospects are full-service bank competitors and scaled independent advisors, M&A downturn potential, IPO market languishing.

Lincoln’s Recent Financial Results

LCLN has produced strong topline revenue growth, although Q4 2025 received a bump from its MarshBerry acquisition closing during the final quarter of the year.

Operating margin has been above public peers while cash flow from operations rose substantially in 2025, as the table shows below.

Lincoln’s IPO Plan

LCLN plans to offer the public its Class A shares in an IPO that could exceed $250 million, excluding customary underwriter allotments.

The company and shareholder share sale lock-ups will be for the standard 180-day period following the effective date of the SEC-approved IPO prospectus.

Net proceeds of the IPO will be used as described below:

We intend to cause LILP to use the net proceeds from the sale of common units to us [i] to redeem [an as-yet undisclosed amount of] common units held by certain LILP Partners, [an as-yet undisclosed amount of] which will be received by certain of our directors and executive officers, [ii] to pay fees and expenses of approximately [an as-yet undisclosed amount] in connection with this offering and the other Organizational Transactions, and [iii] to repay [an as-yet undisclosed amount] under the Credit Facilities and [iv] any remaining proceeds for general corporate purposes. Our management will have broad discretion to direct LILP’s use of the proceeds.

(Source: SEC)

Management’s online IPO roadshow presentation is not yet available for public viewing.

The company said it isn’t presently the subject of “any material litigation”.

Underwriters for the IPO include Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, BMO Capital Markets, Citizens Capital Markets, Evercore ISI, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods, and Wolfe | Nomura.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of the end of 2025, LCLN had $320.2 million in cash and equivalents and $679.5 million in total liabilities, of which $270.4 million was long-term debt.

The company had net cash of $49.8 million at the end of 2025.

Free cash flow for 2025 amounted to $214.4 million, with only $1.9 million in capital expenditures, so a very light capex intensity, typical of a financial services firm.

The capital allocation from the IPO is mixed between buying some partner interests, repaying some debt and the leftover providing additional working capital.

Lincoln Is Growing Profitably

LCLN hopes to go public and join many of its larger peers, which have access to public markets.

Management’s growth strategy involves expanding on its MarshBerry acquisition, cross-selling services, investing in deeper client relationships across industries and geographies and making strategic acquisitions.

The markets LCLN operates in are in flux. A recent example was the M&A market change from a difficult environment during the previous U.S. administration to a more favorable environment in the current one.

More recently, large M&A activity and advisory fees have seen improvement, while the U.S. IPO market continues to improve in volume after the post-pandemic drop.

In a higher interest rate environment, the market will tend to favor operating profit- and cash flow-generating companies like Lincoln, so the company may have a positive environment in which to float its shares.

Risks for investors in the short term will be unpredictable geopolitical changes, which can whipsaw the stock market, although these events, if they occur, can also provide enticing entry points for nimble investors.

Valuations of public peers are also elevated, so the company may benefit from its initial valuation assumptions.

My overall summary of the LCLN IPO is:

  • Positives - Topline growth from organic and acquisition activity, strong financial results

  • Uncertain - Cyclical business characteristics, intense competition

  • Negatives - A portion of the IPO proceeds used for partner redemptions & debt paydown, controlled company structure, tax receivable agreement liabilities

Institutional investors will likely produce demand for the IPO, assuming reasonable pricing assumptions from the company.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: Shell's $16 Billion ARC Deal Strengthens The Thesis
data.description:
Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) reported Q4 2025 and full-year results on March 4, and the results delivered on every box I asked it to check in my previous article.

For one, production hit a record. Reserves grew to a record high. And, operating costs fell yet again. Meanwhile, the Peace River sale closed and has cleaned up the balance sheet even more.

That's just the beginning of the story, though. I think the bigger story now is what has happened down the road in Calgary, as Shell (SHEL) will buy Tourmaline's rival, ARC Resources (AETUF), for $16.4 billion.

That's the largest single transaction in the Canadian gas patch in years. ARC is a quality asset, but it's smaller than Tourmaline by about $7 billion in market cap and produces a fraction of the gas. If Shell is paying full price to fix its reserves problem with ARC, the implied bid for the largest, lowest-cost, longest-life Canadian gas producer ought to be much higher.

Q4 2025 Was A Good Quarter, To Start

So, what happened last quarter? The results were solid in my view. Tourmaline's production of 659,204 barrels of oil per day (equivalent) was strong while January finished over 685,000, tracking ahead of the Q4 guidance range I had previously flagged in my February coverage; this puts Tourmaline on track to meet its 700,000 boepd target.

The reserve number is what stood out most to me. Tourmaline added 829 million boe of 2P reserves in 2025, including a corporate record 457 million boe of organic adds, both after backing out 2025 production. Now its 2P base is above 6 billion boe, and reserve life stands at 26 years. For context, that's an additional five years longer than the figure in February.

Operating costs were $4.66 per barrel of oil (equivalent) in Q4, down over 9% from the first half of 2025. Management continues to aim for a target of an additional $1.50/boe reduction by 2031; this would lead to approximately $500 million in annual savings, if achieved.

Tourmaline also pulled $350 million out of the 2026 E&P capex plan to maximize free cash flow. Its dividend was held at $.50 per quarter. The forward yield is 4.66%, comfortably above where I'd want to own a Canadian gas producer with this kind of growth profile.

The Balance Sheet Just Keeps Getting Stronger

The Peace River High sale to Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) closed in February for $765 million in proceeds.

While that figure came in below the estimates I had, and below high-end analysts estimates at the time, it's still a good deal for Tourmaline, which used the proceeds to reduce its net debt further. Its year-end net debt finished at $1.5 billion (roughly .45x its 2026 cash flow), down from $2.3 billion at the end of Q3 2025.

On March 12, Tourmaline took another step to further improve its balance sheet, issuing $250 million of senior unsecured notes due 2031 at 3.934%. That's investment-grade paper at a sub-4% coupon, and obviously very favorable terms, and I think it tells you how the credit market feels about Tourmaline.

When you combine this bond raise with the proceeds from the Peace River sale, and the ongoing capex cuts, Tourmaline's financial position is growing only stronger, and arguably is in the best financial position of any North American natural gas producer. That's important for any company that might be looking to acquire Tourmaline - or if Tourmaline goes on the offensive and acquire someone.

The company can maintain such a robust balance sheet thanks to its strong cash flow. Tourmaline is guiding for approximately $4 billion of post-tax cash flow vs. $2.55 billion of E&P capital and $775 million of base dividends, leaving ~$915 million free cash flow that it can use for special dividends, share buybacks, exploration, or further debt reduction. Both the maintenance capex and the base dividend are covered at gas prices as low as $2.15 per mcf NYMEX, which is well below the current strip.

With net debt at $1.5 billion (0.45x 2026 cash flow), the lowest leverage ratio in its peer group, and roughly $3 billion of available liquidity post-PRH sale and its latest bond issuance, Tourmaline has a ton of flexibility here. It can fund organic growth toward the 850,000 boepd target by 2031, layer on a $3-5 billion-plus acquisition, increase special dividends, etc.

Insiders Keep Buying, Aggressively

Insider buying is just icing on the cake to an already strong investment thesis.

In my prior coverage, I flagged that its CEO, Mike Rose, was a big buyer of stock throughout H2 2025. Rose hasn't stopped, though. Just last week, Rose added another 5,000 shares for just under $300,000.

Zoom out, and the picture is even better. Over the past three months, Tourmaline insiders have logged 12 separate purchases and zero sales. Total purchases run to roughly $20 million across the group. I love seeing it.

I've tracked insider activity in the resource sector for over a decade. There are many reasons an insider might sell. There's only one reason a CEO with a billion-dollar personal stake keeps writing six-figure checks for more stock. He thinks it's cheap.

It's worth noting that Rose has done this dance twice before. He sold Berkley Petroleum to Anadarko in 2001 for C$1.5 billion. He sold Duvernay Oil to Shell in 2008 for C$5.9 billion at a 40% premium. He's not buying for the dividend. In my view, he's buying because he thinks Tourmaline is worth a lot more than the market is paying.

Shell's ARC Deal Is The Read-Through

Now to the headline catalyst.

Shell announced the acquisition of ARC Resources for approximately $16.4 billion. ARC's portfolio is roughly 60% natural gas and 40% liquids. The transaction adds about 2 billion boe to Shell's reserve base, with expected synergies of roughly $250 million per year and approximately $1.5 billion per year of incremental free cash flow.

The strategic logic is straightforward. Shell's reserves have been declining. Buying ARC is the cleanest, fastest way to reset the reserves base while picking up high-quality Montney exposure that flows directly into LNG Canada, which Shell already operates. ARC is upstream gas. LNG Canada is the downstream. The vertical integration is obvious.

Now stack Tourmaline up against ARC. Tourmaline produces over 685,000 boepd against ARC's 408,000 boepd. Tourmaline carries 6 billion boe of 2P reserves to ARC's 2.3 billion. Tourmaline's drilling inventory runs 75-plus years versus 20 years for ARC (according to page 18 of its presentation). Tourmaline owns the fourth-largest gas processing and midstream business that ARC doesn't have. If you look at slide 3 of the Tourmaline corporate presentation (March 2026), its natural gas production sits above CNQ and just below Ovintiv (OVV). It's the largest pure-play gas producer in Canada and the fifth-largest in North America.

If Shell is paying a 20% premium for ARC's smaller, less efficient platform, what's the right price for the largest, lowest-cost, longest-life Canadian gas producer? I don't think Shell takes another swing this big in Canada any time soon. But ExxonMobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), BP (BP), all have reasons to want more West Coast LNG exposure, and several Asian strategic buyers have been dipping toes in the basin through joint ventures and supply contracts.

Tourmaline is the most attractive takeout candidate in the Canadian patch. And if no one calls, Mike Rose has a very long track record of going on offense himself.

Has Tourmaline Signaled its Open to a Sale?

The simple answer is no, at least not directly. There's no "for sale" sign on this company's website, per say - it hasn't run a process to seek a buyer, and there haven't been any public comments I'm aware of that would indicate that its shopping the company.

But, take a look at the company's background. Its CEO Mike Rose has spent the past 16 years building Tourmaline into the company it is today, and on multiple earnings calls he has framed M&A this way - that he'll evaluate any transaction that's accretive to shareholders, whether Tourmaline is the buyer or the seller.

"We went through like 5 years of putting primarily the BC Montney gas complex together through or expanding it through a whole series of acquisitions from COVID on. And we have put in place now the BC build-out infrastructure for the next 5 or 6 years. Now we're going to go realize all the upside and all the value from those really well-timed acquisitions. So we'll always look at perhaps small asset tuck-ins. But right now, it's....the focus is much more on organic growth from the extensive inventories we have really in both gas complexes." - Mike Rose in Q3 2025 earnings call.

Rose's history is also hard to ignore, as he previously sold Berkley to Anadarko in 2001 and Duvernay to Shell in 2008 at a 40% premium. Both deals were exits to majors looking to bulk up Western Canadian gas reserves.

The recent capital allocation moves also point to a company sharpening its profile, and potentially seeking M&A. Selling Peace River High to CNRL, refinancing debt, cutting 2026 capex by $350 million, all create flexibility.

My take is that Tourmaline is not actively shopping itself. But the board would certainly evaluate any serious offer at a meaningful premium, which I view as a real possibility. Tourmaline looks to be in the conversation following the Shell-ARC deal. Even if no offer ever comes, shareholders are getting a high-quality, low-cost, high-growth Canadian gas producer compounding at a very reasonable valuation, so it's worth buying shares regardless.

Valuation

Tourmaline trades at roughly 6.61 times its forward price-to-cash-flow and 6.38 times forward EV/EBITDA. That's not deeply undervalued by historical standards, or compared to oil and gas peers. But I'd argue that it's more than a fair price for a company of Tourmaline's quality, with its low operating costs and pristine balance sheet, longest reserve life in the basin, and a 4.66% dividend yield while you wait for growth to come.

You're also paying for strategic exposure to high-premium international LNG markets. Tourmaline has roughly 213,000 mmbtu/d of 2026 exposure to international pricing benchmarks like JKM and TTF, which have historically been clear at multiples of AECO. That marketing book is a real asset, not a footnote.

Peers like ARC, Peyto Exploration (PEYUF), and Whitecap Resources (WCPRF) all trade in roughly the same neighborhood today on EV/EBITDA, but none has Tourmaline's combination of scale, reserve life, and balance sheet.

Risks

Lower natural gas prices is an obvious risk, but I'd argue that gas prices have already been weak, so the downside is relatively limited.

There's obviously no guarantee of any M&A with Tourmaline, and there's the risk that it does not properly allocate its capital (or, ends up being acquired in an unfriendly deal that doesn't benefit shareholders). Tourmaline has plenty of dry powder. If Rose pulls the trigger on a large acquisition that the market doesn't love, the stock could suffer.

And the takeout thesis only matters if a buyer shows up. The Shell-ARC deal puts Tourmaline squarely in the strategic conversation, but base case here is that Tourmaline keeps compounding on its own and the market eventually catches up.

Bottom Line

Tourmaline delivered everything I asked for in Q4. Production, reserves, costs, dividend, balance sheet, all moving in the right direction. Insiders are buying with both hands. And Shell just put a $16.4 billion floor under what large-cap Canadian gas assets are worth.

I'm reiterating my Strong Buy. Tourmaline, along with Expand Energy (EXE), remain my top 2 natural gas stock picks, but I favor Tourmaline over Expand currently. For long-term investors wanting exposure to natural gas, Tourmaline is arguably the safest way to play it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ennis: Some Parts Will Continue To Decline, While Others Have A Chance
data.description:
Ennis (EBF) is a U.S. manufacturer and wholesaler of printed business products, selling through a national distributor network instead of directly to consumers. EBF’s core model is a private-label, acquisition-led print platform that focuses on business forms and commercial documents and has over 50 facilities.

The company makes and sells products like business forms, checks, commercial printing, envelopes, tags, labels, plastic cards, and multimedia packaging. Ennis supplies distributors, printers, software vendors, and direct-mail firms that need customized print products on a recurring basis.

Briefly On Performance

We can see that over the last three years, the company’s top-line performance was quite bad. It was in a clear downtrend due to its core print market softness. The demand for traditional business documents declined, while pricing became a lot more competitive. This sort of performance matches a company that is in a mature, shrinking category, and I’m afraid the long-term trajectory points to consistent erosion of demand.

We can see that the company’s revenues found a bottom sometime in mid-'25, showing some sort of stabilization, as the company had already absorbed much of the integration disruptions and was able to lean on its national distributor base. A normalization after a period of demand erosion. Not a strong growth demand.

In the latest quarter, Q4 '25, we can see that the company’s revenues grew 4% y/y, beating analysts by $1.26 million. So, at least there was some growth in the last quarter, which helped its performance.

Going over to the company’s efficiency and profitability, we can see that the company’s operations were quite resilient despite the supposed cost price competition. I was expecting to see massive profitability deterioration, but that was not the case. Over the last three years, the management kept the company’s profitability quite consistent despite the decline in revenues. In the latest quarter, GAAP EPS came in at $0.35, which was flat compared to the same time last year. It certainly isn’t anything to write home about, but it is better than a decline.

Looking at other important efficiency metrics, we can see that the management is doing a commendable job at utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital rather efficiently, which should create value. Also, I am impressed that the company’s ROTC is over 10%, which tells me the company overall has a decent moat and pricing power in the business.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. As of Q4 ’25, EBF had around $34.5 million in cash and equivalents and no debt, which is a great position to be in all the time. I don’t think having debt on the books is automatically a bad sign, but many investors do tend to avoid it like the plague. Also, the company’s cash flow from operating activities was still quite healthy at around $52.7 million, giving the company a CFO margin of around 13.4%. The company continues to be a cash-generating machine, which is expected from a mature company.

Comments On The Outlook

There isn’t a lot to talk about in terms of where the company is heading. I would like to see the company focus on three things, which have been positive developments for the company. I would almost call it a dying business. I would like to see the company continue to generate steady cash flow going forward. One way to have this done is to keep the business itself structurally simple. Keep the distributor-led model, keep the balance sheet clean, and use acquisitions and other cost controls to squeeze more free cash flow out of a slow-growth base.

Ennis should not be looking to reinvent the business. It just needs to do what it has done in the past, which is to preserve its niche and add accretive bolt-on acquisitions to let scale, mix, and operational discipline gradually lift its cash flow.

That way, the company can maintain its margins or even increase them as time goes on. I may have jumped the gun in saying that the business is dying, but it certainly is in a decline, and that is due to legacy printing category shrinkage. I don’t think the whole industry is doomed; some parts I see will always be resilient going forward. Those parts include labels and packaging. These are always going to be tied to physical goods, shipping, compliance, and, probably most importantly, customization. This makes them a lot less exposed to pure paperless substitution. The traditional business forms are the weakest because of digitization, which keeps eating away at volume. That cannot be changed, and so the business needs to adapt and focus on what will most likely survive this. I’m glad that the company is doing exactly that, because EBF lists labels, tags, specialty packaging, and secure documents as its main products that will most likely always be in demand. The acquisition of CFC Print & Mail will help EBF to pivot to a mix shift that is a lot more resilient.

So, in short, the company needs to continue to survive, and the way to do it is to keep acquiring strategic companies that will aid its pivot into more resilient businesses that will continue to be relevant in the long run. Businesses that will help it become a better cash compounder and improve its profitability as its dying segments disappear. Unfortunately, these segments still make up quite a big chunk of the overall revenues of EBF, so there might be further downside to be had until they become irrelevant.

Should You Buy Ennis?

Let’s take a look at a quick DCF model to get an idea of what the company is worth currently. Below are the assumptions I’ve used.

I think revenue is as good as it can get right now, because it has been mostly on a decline, and I may still be a bit too generous.

For margins, I am increasing them slightly as the price mix goes towards higher value, less legacy products. I don’t think we will see a massive margin improvement, mainly because that is the nature of the business, and we saw how steady and consistent the profitability has been in the recent past. The rest are as close to the company’s historical averages as I can find. We get the following projections with these assumptions.

Additionally, I went with 8.5% as my discount rate because the company’s low 6% WACC is not enough of a margin of safety. Also, the terminal growth rate I set it at is 1.5%. 2.5% seems too high. So, we see that the company’s fair value is around $20.32, which means the company is trading at its intrinsic value.

I wouldn’t recommend starting a position at these prices and would wait for another pullback, which I am sure is going to happen before the company pivots towards more resilient products and sheds the legacy printing business completely. I believe there is still growth for the company in the future, and I don’t think it is going to go away anytime soon, but it’s a growth that I am not particularly excited about.

data.title: Gold Still Sets The Tone For Silver's Next Move
data.description:
Intro

I’m likely not the only one who has been eyeing silver’s impressive chart lately and wondering how I missed a move that now seems so obvious. But as we know, charts always look plain and simple in hindsight. In the middle of the actual action, however, there’s a mix of emotions and all sorts of confusing signals that often make it hard to stay objective and pull the trigger at the right time.

I have to admit, I’ve long been more excited about gold—viewing it as the ultimate symbol of money and wealth since ancient times. I’ve delved deep into its dynamics, even writing an extensive analysis last November: From Ancient Times To Bretton Woods And On To Bitcoin: Why Gold Still Matters. However, I’ve never examined the drivers behind silver’s movements in more detail. Given the recent dramatic surge to all-time highs and the once-unthinkable breach of the $100 mark, I felt this was the right moment to unpack silver's perspectives.

My goal is to understand whether the current setup—given that silver has fallen nearly 40% from its all-time highs—is more like a consolidation before another leg higher, or if it’s reasonable to expect an extended drawdown and stay on the sidelines this time.

Silver Is Tightly Correlated with Gold

For me, silver has always been something of a satellite to gold—or its younger brother, if you will. Throughout history, silver coins have played a crucial role whenever kings and rulers of the era sought to expand the monetary base once their vaults had been emptied of gold. In a sense, they have been fellow money equivalents for centuries. One could even say that silver has historically served as a vehicle for "Quantitative Easing" (QE) for those in power, much like the debasement of silver denarii in the Roman Empire when funds ran dry. When gold supply is tight, silver saves the day; I think it’s easy to draw various parallels to the present.

Latest Moves in Gold and Silver

I believe we can draw the starting line of the current gold (XAUUSD:CUR) rally to early 2024, when it decisively broke above $2,000 and, more importantly, managed to hold that level and begin its trend higher.

During this period, silver prices (XAGUSD:CUR) closely tracked the movements of gold, consistently aligning with its trends, until experiencing a significant surge at the end of 2025:

After reviewing past rallies, it appears typical for gold and silver to move in tandem—until silver suddenly surges ahead, often outpacing gold by a wide margin. This time, silver has outperformed gold by as much as nearly 100%.

Even a quick look at comparative charts going back to the 1980s shows the same pattern: while the two generally move in similar patterns, silver’s swings are far more aggressive, with much sharper bursts than gold.

We can identify the two most recent major cycles to examine more closely. The first, longer and stronger one began around 2008 during the subprime crisis and gradually cooled off by 2015. The second, milder one emerged during the COVID-era easing period, roughly from 2020 through 2023.

As seen in the first chart below, gold and silver initially moved mostly together in 2008. But at the start of 2009, gold’s rise accelerated while silver lagged behind for years before finally catching up during the QE‑driven surge in spring 2011. Silver then dramatically overshot gold — but the subsequent drawdown was just as sharp, with both metals eventually cooling off and spending the following years trending lower in nearly the same pattern.

The recent spike prior to the one we’re seeing now occurred roughly between 2020 and 2023. It followed almost the same pattern: in this cycle, silver started out stronger, gold eventually caught up and took the lead, silver followed with force, and then spiked ahead — even managing to stay above gold for a longer stretch. But just like in the previous cycle, both metals ultimately cooled off and ended the move by trending lower in sync.

…and here we have the current rally. Once again, the two traded nearly in sync until gold took the lead during last April’s tariff‑driven selloff. They then realigned, and silver surged toward the end of 2025. The cooling phase shown in the chart follows a very similar pattern so far.

To get an even stronger sense of just how massive the current rally has been, it’s useful to look at the next charts, which show the overwhelming surge in trading volumes that led to the all‑time highs in both gold and silver. The chart also shows that silver’s trading volumes have only recently risen to levels comparable to gold.

To add more context to these movements, it’s also worth taking a look at the gold and silver volatility chart, which shows that silver has almost always exhibited significantly higher volatility than gold, with only brief exceptions during 2000–2003 when the relationship was more mixed.

So to me, these cycles show that the two shiny metals are tightly correlated, but gold consistently plays the role of the older brother — the frontrunner, the one who sets the tone, if you will — as it has throughout human monetary history. When gold begins to move, it often takes some time for silver to catch up, but once it does, it tends to overshoot sharply. That overshoot is usually followed by a return to normality, with silver giving back its elevated price levels during easing phases. Even so, the broader picture remains the same: they generally move hand in hand — it’s just that the younger brother has a far more temperamental streak that reveals itself from time to time.

After the Recent Gold and Silver Rally — What Might Come Next

All of the above leads me to a simple conclusion: we can’t assess silver’s outlook in isolation. For silver to rally meaningfully from here, history’s playbook suggests it still needs a positive catalyst from gold’s price action. Silver has almost never staged a sustained rally without gold taking the strong lead.

What the Fundamentals Say

Many market observers point to silver’s real‑world physical demand as the basis for its elevated price action. According to the Silver Institute, the silver market is in a structural deficit:

The next graph shows where the demand is coming from. We can observe that as historical photographic demand has diminished, jewelry, electronics, and batteries have largely filled the gap. The trend appears encouraging and could support a continued rise in overall demand:

While it’s hard to find solid research that clearly and in detail proves, for example, that solar panels or other sectors will drive exponential growth in silver demand, I did come across one study that, in my view, provides credible evidence that the automotive industry will need more silver going forward. That said, we still can’t describe the demand outlook as explosive from any perspective.

The Silver Institute also offers a detailed breakdown of silver’s supply and demand:

As we can see, supply has increased in 2025 compared to 2024, while demand from real‑world industrial use and jewelry is on the decline. The only significant increase in demand is coming from the Coin & Net Bar segment. Setting that source of demand aside, it shows there’s roughly a 177‑million‑ounce surplus in terms of actual real‑world usage.

For me, it seems fair to conclude that, however we look at this supply‑and‑demand outline, silver’s price is not fully supported by real‑world consumption but is instead meaningfully influenced by investor and speculative sentiment.

Outlook for Silver in 2026

Silver Institute provides their view:

Total demand this year is forecast to fall modestly by 2 percent to 1.11 Boz. Double-digit losses are expected in jewelry and silverware, as the impact of higher prices continues. Industrial demand is projected to decline by 3 percent, chiefly due to a further and marked slowdown in PV offtake. Some of these losses will be mitigated by firmer coin and net bar demand, which is expected to jump by 18 percent.

Global silver mine production is expected to remain flat in 2026. Broader grade-related and operational pressures across key producing regions should offset modest growth at a limited number of assets. With mine production stable this year, we expect the structural market deficit to widen to 46.3 Moz.

To sum up, there’s nothing particularly encouraging here — real‑world usage is declining, and the demand story increasingly relies on investment flows.

Ways to Gain Exposure to Silver’s Price Moves

While there are plenty of ways for investors to get exposure to physical silver on an exchange, including through futures or ETFs like SLV, PSLV, or SIVR, there are also equity‑based alternatives. The Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) features a globally diversified mix of mining companies with strong exposure to silver. Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF - (SILJ) is broadly similar but has a somewhat larger variety of miners and is more diversified. Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) offers a blended approach, combining physical silver with an 18% stake in PSLV and exposure to silver mining companies as well.

The Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) Holdings:

Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ) Holdings:

Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) Holdings:

For seasoned traders, there is also the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (AGQ), which offers 2x leverage and provides additional upside optionality during silver’s wild price swings.

ProShares, a fund manager, presents its offering as follows:

AGQ seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to two times (2x) the daily performance of the Bloomberg Silver SubindexSM.

For experienced traders, AGQ is a great vehicle to trade, delivering significantly more bang for the buck — but as a leveraged ETF, its risks can’t be ignored. The fund manager warns:

While the Fund has a daily investment objective, you may hold Fund shares for longer than one day if you believe it is consistent with your goals and risk tolerance. For any holding period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Daily Target. These differences may be significant. Smaller index gains/losses and higher index volatility contribute to returns worse than the Daily Target. Larger index gains/losses and lower index volatility contribute to returns better than the Daily Target.

The more extreme these factors are, the more they occur together, and the longer your holding period while these factors apply, the more your return will tend to deviate. Investors should consider periodically monitoring their geared fund investments in light of their goals and risk tolerance.

AGQ invests in futures and swap and does not directly invest in silver.

Takeaway

Times are uncertain, and silver is undoubtedly an asset class that can serve both as a tool for portfolio diversification and—for those comfortable with sharper price swings—a vehicle for long-term capital preservation.

Silver’s real-world demand is clearly present, but the widely discussed structural deficit still depends more on investors' willingness to allocate capital than on a meaningful increase in industrial usage at the moment.

In my opinion, silver cannot be viewed in isolation from the broader investor appetite for gold, which has historically acted as the leading indicator for silver’s most decisive moves. In today’s geopolitically, monetarily, and fiscally uncertain environment, there are plenty of well-reasoned arguments supporting gold—and by extension, silver. That said, for silver to rally meaningfully from here, in my view, gold needs to re-establish its strong uptrend as an essential precondition.

As for my stance, I remain neutral—silver is a 'Hold' for me at the moment. History shows that after powerful spikes, it often moves sideways for a while or even drifts lower for an extended period. That said, for seasoned traders, silver continues to offer excellent opportunities for high-conviction setups.

data.title: Pinterest Is Likely Dead Money (Q1 Preview)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Pinterest (PINS) at the end of December, I downgraded the stock to a “hold” as management guided for revenue to slow down in Q4, which tends to be their seasonally strongest time of the year. Meanwhile, a slowdown in the growth rate of ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) was also concerning to me, especially when far larger and deep-pocketed players like Meta (META) and Google (GOOG) have been growing their ad revenues at a much faster rate.

As I put it, “Pinterest management needs to present a bold new plan to revive growth back in the company, especially in its US & Canada regions. Until that happens, I believe investor sentiment will remain depressed and the stock price will be range-bound.”

That has exactly been the case, as the company failed to impress investors in their Q4 FY25 earnings in February, missing both top- and bottom-line estimates, from ARPU slowing down even further from weakness in ad pricing.

While management acknowledged their lackluster earnings and committed to broadening their advertiser base beyond large retail advertisers, which had slashed their ad budgets in the face of higher tariffs, they failed to provide a clear timeline as to when we can expect a potential inflection may occur.

Pinterest stock is down 23% since the time of my previous writing, hugely underperforming the S&P 500.

The company is slated to report its Q1 FY26 earnings on 05/04, where both revenue and earnings are expected to decline even further. The way I see it, even if Pinterest manages to beat guidance, I wouldn’t expect a durable rerating in the stock until we get clear evidence of top-line acceleration.

In the meantime, the stock does not even present an attractive risk-reward when looking at Wall Street price targets. As a result, in the absence of a clear timeline on potential revenue inflection, along with net-negative risk reward, I will downgrade the stock to a “sell” ahead of its Q1 earnings.

A Quick Recap Of Pinterest’s Q4 FY25

When Pinterest reported its Q4 FY25 earnings in February, the company missed both its revenue and earnings expectations.

On the revenue front, Pinterest saw its two-line growth rate slow 300 basis points sequentially to 14% YoY, generating $1.3B in revenue. This was the case, as the revenue growth rate in both Europe and Rest of World declined on a sequential basis. During the earnings call, management acknowledged their less than satisfactory results and attributed the weakness to tariffs affecting the ad budgets of major retailers, particularly in home furnishings and decor. CEO William Reddy also stressed the importance of broadening their revenue mix and discussed their three strategic priorities that are centered on “building a differential visual search, discovery, and shopping experience; keeping AI at the core of their product innovation strategy; and accelerating monetization through improved go-to-market and sales execution.”

The good news is that Pinterest’s MAUs (Monthly Active Users) continued to grow at a steady rate during the quarter, up 9% YoY to 619M users, with strength across all its geographies, as can be seen below.

However, the problem lies in the growth rate of ARPU, which was up just 2%, compared to 5% in Q3, as ad pricing weakened from their largest retail advertisers pulling back on their ad budgets.

In the meantime, the management is trying to compensate for their slowing growth by tightening their profitability parameters. As you can see below, the cost of revenue has been trending lower, along with R&D (Research & Development) and S&M (Sales & Marketing) expenses also declining as a percentage of revenue.

What investors want to see is an inflection in their ad revenue growth as management looks to diversify their advertisers by accelerating the growth in SMB and mid-market segments. Unfortunately, management failed to provide clarity on the timeline of their potential ad revenue inflection and margin implications of new investments.

What To Expect In Q1?

Looking forward, Pinterest is slated to report its Q1 FY26 earnings on 05/04, when revenue is expected to further slow to a 13.17% YoY growth rate during the quarter. What is even more concerning is that forward consensus estimates show no signs of inflection in revenue growth at any point in time in the coming quarters, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings per share estimates, Pinterest is expected to see its Q1 EPS decline 4.6% YoY to $0.22 per share. But, based on consensus estimates, Q1 should mark a bottom, with the growth rate in earnings per share improving after that.

The competitive landscape is also not favorable to Pinterest, where far larger players like Meta and Google are projected to grow their ad revenues at a faster rate in FY26, by 24% and 12%, respectively, with superior margins, indicating significant market share loss and competitive moat erosion for Pinterest.

I’m Not Expecting Any Rerating Soon

There is no doubt that Pinterest stock is “cheap” at the moment, trading at a forward PE ratio of 11.07, 18% lower than the sector median and 63% lower than its 5-year average PE ratio.

However, until we see a meaningful reacceleration in the company’s top-line growth, I don’t expect a meaningful re-rating in the stock. In other words, Pinterest is likely going to be “dead money” until then.

Plus, I look at Wall Street price target estimates; the risk-reward does not look attractive, despite the stock trading at record low valuations. At the moment, the average price target on Wall Street is $23.43, representing a potential upside of 18% from current levels. However, when looking at the “low” price target of $15.40 per share, there is a downside risk of 22% from current levels.

In other words, investing at current levels does not present a net positive risk-reward, along with management providing no guidelines of potential inflection timelines, thus further depressing investor sentiment in the stock.

My final verdict and conclusions

Pinterest is likely “dead money” for now. As a previous investor, I got out of the name in January at a loss of 22% and will stay away from the stock, as the company has clearly lost the plot at the moment, while risk-reward is not attractive either.

Meanwhile, I will downgrade the stock to a “sell” ahead of its Q1 earnings.

data.title: Brinker: Oil Prices A Lower Risk As Chili's Boom Continues In Q3
data.description:
Shares of Brinker International (EAT) have been a poor performer over the past year. While the company has actually reported strong results thanks to a remarkable turnaround at its Chili’s brand, investors have soured on restaurant stocks over fears about discretionary spending. Strong quarterly earnings reported Wednesday morning may help to dispel these fears, and shares jumped 10% in early trading. I last covered Brinker in January, but this call has not played out with shares down about 8% since then. At the time, I saw the outlook brightening for restaurants, but the Iran War has thrown this into doubt. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit EAT.

In the company’s third quarter, Brinker earned $2.90, which beat estimates by $0.04 as revenue grew 3% to $1.5 billion. EPS grew 9% from last year thanks to income growth and the benefits of its buyback program. Same-restaurant sales continue to perform fairly well, up 3.3%, though there remains a large divergence between brands. Management described demand as “strong,” pointing to a stronger consumer than other companies like Domino’s (DPZ).

Adjusted EBITDA rose about 1.5% to $224 million, and operating income was up 6% to $167 million. Operating margins expanded by 30bps to 11.3%, thanks primarily to lower corporate costs. Restaurant-level EBITDA margins actually declined by 50bps to 18.4%, given one-time repair expenses and higher labor costs. Operating margins benefit from lower corporate overhead as a share of revenue thanks to ongoing growth of the top line, a margin benefit that should persist into fiscal 2027.

Chili's leads; Maggiano lags

Drilling into segment results, Chili’s same-restaurant sales rose a solid 4%, a 20th straight quarter of growth. With $1.36 billion of sales, Chili’s is over 90% of the business, so its performance is what matters most to EAT. Comparable sales growth is decelerating here after a period of double-digit growth, reflecting a much more difficult comparison. Last year, comparable sales jumped 31%, and that pace of growth is not sustainable at a restaurant (eventually you run out of tables and hit capacity). Indeed, growing further on top of last year’s growth is very impressive.

There has been concern that the jump in oil prices and geopolitical uncertainty would lead consumers to pull back. While a definite risk, this is not happening. Comps were up just 0.6% in January (weighed down by a winter storm), and then they rose 5.9% in both February and March when oil prices began their surge. Chili’s has returned to the forefront of consumers’ minds, and its value pricing has helped it weather this shock so far.

Maggiano’s continues to struggle as Brinker is in the early innings of its turnaround plan, bringing this chain back to its roots, similar to what it did for Chili’s. Comparable sales fell 4.6%. This brand is just $107 million in sales, so it is less consequential to results. Operating income fell 60% to $5 million due to lower sales and margin pressure. Maggiano’s has brought back old favorites to the menu, refocused on service quality, and enhanced portions. Just as these efforts worked to turn around Chili’s to remarkable success, I expect Maggiano’s to bottom later this year, though I expect the pace of recovery to be slower.

Macro was improving before Iran

Looking at the macro backdrop, my view entering the year was that the macro environment was improving for restaurants, at least before the Iran War. This seems to have been playing out. As you can see below, restaurant sales inflected higher in February with 61% of respondents reporting growth, the highest reading in over a year. Larger tax refunds should be a tailwind for consumer spending in 2026, all else equal, and higher grocery prices have made restaurant pricing relatively more attractive. The question is to what extent oil derails this story; I believe the impact is less than feared.

Gas prices are a manageable risk

While there is this perceived risk that gasoline prices will hurt spending at restaurants, the data actually does not back that up. Looking from 2000-2020 (COVID restaurant closures and reopening heavily distort the 2020-2022 period), it is interesting to see that spikes in oil prices in 2003, 2007, 2010, and 2017 do not coincide with drops in restaurant spending. If anything, spending at the pump and at restaurants generally move in the same direction, with one notable exception. During 2014-2015, when oil prices plunged, consumers used those savings to spend more at restaurants.

This is an interesting phenomenon that is comforting when considering the outlook for Brinker with oil prices above $100 (and likely to remain elevated for some time in my view). Now, increased gas prices force consumers to either save less or spend less on other items. The outcome likely depends on the income level of the consumer; higher-income consumers with greater savings and buffers are likely to just save less, and the pressure is likely to be more pronounced among lower-income consumers, a cohort that already spends less at restaurants on average.

I see travel as facing more pressure, and consumers may actually use those savings to spend a bit more while at home, providing support for restaurant traffic. I also expect consumers to prioritize value when going out, and Brinker’s brands are well positioned for this possibility. The impact of higher oil prices on consumer spending remains a key risk to monitor, but given historic data, it may not be as concerning as first thought.

Cash flow enables capital returns

Turning back to Brinker, so far this year, the company has generated just under $400 million of free cash flow, despite $10 million of working capital pressure. This is up from $308 million last year, thanks to stronger profitability. During the quarter, EAT repaid the balance on its revolver, and it also bought back $108 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 4% from last year. The company has a stellar balance sheet with just $424 million of long-term finance leases and debt. Given this balance sheet, Brinker can direct all free cash flow to shareholder returns in the form of buybacks.

Alongside results, management tightened EPS guidance to the upper end of the prior range, at $10.60-$10.85, while lowering its cap-ex outlook by $10 million to about $245 million. Back in January, I was targeting at least $10.10 in EPS and $450 million in free cash flow. This view was a bit too conservative, and I expect EPS to be in the upper half of the guided range while free cash flow should be at least $475 million, depending on working capital movements.

Shares have upside past $160

Looking forward to fiscal 2027, it is still early, but I expect mid-single-digit growth at Chili’s, low single-digit declines at Maggiano’s, and about a 5% decline in the share count. This should support 10-15% growth in earnings or $12-$12.50 in EPS. Even at $140, shares are less than 12x forward earnings when peers like Darden (DRI) trade closer to 18x. Brinker is smaller and less diversified, but its primary brand has significant momentum.

As investors come to appreciate that oil prices are not as problematic as feared, we should see shares re-rate higher. I believe shares should trade at least to a 7% forward free cash flow yield, which still discounts just 3% long-term growth to generate a 10% return. As such, I would be a buyer of EAT up to $160, and I view this target as conservative. With over 15% of further upside, I reiterate EAT as a “buy.”

data.title: Pulse Seismic Inc: A Stronger Yielder Benefitting From Increasing Western Canadian Activity
data.description:
Pulse Seismic Inc. (PSD:CA) is the owner of the largest bank of 2D and 3D seismic data in Western Canada. The primary users of this data are oil and gas companies, looking to gain information on new areas, reinterpretations of existing structures and new crown mineral locations, among others. The O&G industry has come a long way since the wildcatting days, with a focus on more efficient outcomes both from exploration and producing wells. Seismic data has helped to dramatically improve the efficiency, giving users of the data better detail on a large scale and with precision when required. Pulse Seismic has developed a substantial library of this data in a fossil fuel rich area that it has been able to monetize through several different mechanisms. This article looks at Pulse Seismic’s business as well as the current macro environment it is operating in.

The Company’s Seismic Data

The company has both 2D and 3D seismic data in its library. 2D gives a wider mapping level to get an idea of geological formations. 3D data goes into further detail than 2D data but is focused on a smaller area. The company largely built its database by acquiring the data during the 2010s with a series of acquisitions.

The value proposition for customers is quite clear; in the past, they may have had to do all their own data gathering for each property a customer may be investigating for economic viability. Rather than needing their own equipment and specialty, they can rent the data only for the relevant area needed. This saves the customer a lot of money. Similarly, Pulse Seismic can then monetize the data as often or for as long as a customer has interest in the properties. While most Pulse’s customers are in the O&G industry, it is not limited to that with customers in several other industries utilizing the data including but not limited to lithium and geothermal.

One of the drivers of use of Pulse’s data is land transactions. As properties go from one party to another, they will want access to the data as they try to re-interpret the data depending on their requirement. Another driver is the building of infrastructure such as pipelines which require this data to incorporate into their engineering specifications.

The Business Model

The simplest is to develop a license agreement with a customer taking a greenfield position in data that is currently under lease. Transactions are a big driver of licensing agreements. In an acquisition/merger, Pulse gives the acquirer 30 days to renegotiate a new deal; in a JV/development a new deal must negotiated and in the case of an asset sale, the licensing agreement does not follow the property to a new owner. These transactions allow Pulse to monetize its data repeatedly and drive revenue from the same assets.

It does not take a lot to manage and administer the business model with administration of contracts and sales being the major drivers, so Pulse supports very healthy margins at roughly 80% of revenue and EbITDA running 76% over the last 5 years.

Financials and Valuation

Pulse posted banner results in 2025. Revenue was $51.1m CAD, EbITDA of $40.8m and shareholder free cash flow (EbITDA less taxes) of $31.6m. These results taken on their own give Pulse some objectively cheap metrics when compared to both the current market cap of $214.3m and enterprise value of $194.6m with an EV to EbITDA under 5x and a price to cash flow of under 7x. This does not really tell the whole story though.

The nature of Pulse’s business is that it has very bulky and volatile revenues, as evidenced below:

The upside is that Pulse has made money every year, even in the trough years and due to its high margins, banner years on the sales front translate directly to the bottom line. For most investors, they like to see steady earnings with regular growth rates. Lumpy earnings like the above make it harder to value.

The company offsets this be returning a substantial amount of capital to its shareholders. This has been primarily done through dividends, but the company also has a buyback in place as well. The regular dividend has been steady since 2021; currently it sits at $0.07 CAD or a 1.7% yield at the current price. However, the company has been doing special dividends most years, with the only exception being 2022 (which was a trough year per the above graph). The 2025 special dividend of $0.20 would bump this yield to over 6% I believe this will continue as the company as 24% insider ownership, making this policy something that clearly aligns with management.

Pulse’s business model is akin to the royalty business models used throughout the natural resource industrial complex, albeit with a more fixed pricing model that is not directly driven by commodity prices. For example, Freehold Royalties (FRU:C) trades at a price to cash flow of 12x and a TTM EV/EbITDA of 12x. This is almost double Pulse’s, but Pulse is performing at near its historical peak financial performance. I do believe that a lot of the reduce multiple and financial performance has been due to the regulatory environment that the natural resource industry has suffered through in Canada over the last decade, but it should be noted that post-COVID, the company’s performance has been trending up.

Looking Forward

It is hard to find a worse regulatory environment for oil and gas than Canada has been over the last decade, despite its natural resource gifts. Development has been impeded at almost every level of government in the country’s pursuit of its environmental goals, despite their small percentage impact compared to many other countries around the world. This has begun to fall with Danielle Smith becoming the new Premier of Alberta which has helped to encourage more exploration and production in Alberta, which has been evident in the up turn in results over the past 5 years. If the Federal government can also start to live up to some of its promises of a more business friendly environment, reduced trade barriers and additional pipelines, Canada could become a much friendlier environment to Pulse’s major customers.

The geopolitical environment has also seen an upturn in oil prices with the uncertainty around the world; energy security is in much greater focus, and higher prices make more properties economic, which would lead to more users of Pulse’s data.

Both these broad macro tailwinds should lead to even better results for Pulse going forward.

Risks

You do not need to look far to see what can happen on the other side of political environment. The disastrous provincial government in British Columbia has brought even basic property rights into question for citizens and business alike, while continuing to throw up roadblocks to pipelines that would benefit Canada as a whole. This is not a large part of Pulse’s business but is certainly not a tailwind.

Artificial intelligence could also be an existential threat if it is able to recreate the data that Pulse has acquired. I suspect that they have very strong data security that keeps this very proprietary information away from LLMs as more companies start to wall off their data in general.

The nature of commodities also means that the other side of the above geopolitical environment means that a soft O&G pricing environment could also impact the amount of activity in the oil patch, which will understandably have a negative impact on Pulse’s financial performance. Fortunately, its cost base gives it a lot of leeway to manage downturns.

The Takeaway

There is a chance for a multiple re-rate if Pulse’s results continue in the trend we saw in 2025, which is certainly possible with a thawing political environment at home in Canada, along with a higher priced oil market driven by current political tensions and potential longer-term effects of the same. Even if a re-rating does not fully occur, the company’s capital return policy will buffer shareholders, who are very aligned with insiders.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!
