data.title: AppLovin: The Bullish Levers Are Still In Place
data.description:
Giving Up Some Of The Sturdy Long-term Gains

AppLovin Corporation (APP), a prominent facilitator of AI-powered advertising solutions, is a stock I’ve been bullish on since September 2024. While APP has witnessed astounding growth (a +4x expansion in its market-cap), since my last coverage, it appears to be going through some wobbles in the current year. To shed more insight, during the first four months of 2026, when the S&P500 is up by almost mid-single-digits, and APP’s tech peers from the S&P500 have more than doubled the returns of the equity benchmark, this ad-tech specialist, has seen a third of its market-cap being shaved off.

Given this recent turbulence, am I still inclined to maintain my bullish stance on the stock? Yes, and here’s why.

Why I Am Still Bullish On AppLovin

Firstly, I believe AppLovin’s recent efforts to broaden into new verticals like e-Commerce could potentially enter a state of inflection this year. At the start of Q4-25, APP had launched its self-serve tool for e-commerce and non-gaming clients to help expedite the onboarding process, which was previously being done manually, and only resulted in a limited pool of clients coming on board (APP has been working on this for the past 1.5 years). A shift from the referral-only state, to a general-availability launch is due to take place before the end of H1-26.

I expect APP to see strong traction from e-commerce advertisers following the general-availability launch because firstly, the former already has an active client base of over 1 billion daily users which gives these e-commerce players access to far wider scale. Secondly, unlike Instagram feeds or Facebook stories, where the attention span of the viewer is quite minimal, the typical average impression time on AppLovin is longer at 35 seconds. Opportunities to double-down on longer-form videos here too are more evident than elsewhere, bringing a very niche pool of e-commerce marketers as well. Separately, it’s also worth noting that e-commerce customers who had initially signed up for Axon Ads in Q4-24, already highlighted a “material increase” in ad spend, 1 year on.

I also suspect that ample investor scrutiny is likely going towards whether APP’s gold-standard EBITDA margins (now at astounding levels of 84%) which is widely seen as one of the principal cogs of most bullish theses on APP. Admittedly, a few naysayers may ponder if APP can hold on to its margin profile as it will likely need to make more investments in headcount and the like, to bolster its models and platform, more so in the face of increased competition from the likes of Unity Software’s (U) AI-powered ad platform-Vector that only went live last year. I’ve previously written about how U’s Vector was already adding great value for its customers in its initial days, but what investors also need to consider is that U can’t hold a candle to APP when it comes to maintaining a lean organization, or extracting value from its employee base.

Already, an employee at APP generates over 15x the revenue (over $6M) that an employee at U generates (less than $0.5M), and this stems from APP’s ethos of not just maintaining a very lean ad-tech team (as validated by the CFO at the MS 2025 TMT Conference), but also because the deployment of their new prospecting ad models (ad campaigns for attracting new customers) aren’t greenlit unless those models can demonstrate higher profitability in real time. This laser focus on profitability means that APP won’t blindly resort to model refreshes until the economics make sense. And after that, it’s not so much about doubling-down on their sales force, but rather, letting the word-of-mouth wave, do the talking.

Then, when it comes to the stock’s valuations, I believe that old adage of “quality doesn’t come cheap” could be applied to a business of APP’s ilk, at least if you’re gauging it through the lens of EBITDA. The last time, I delved into APP it was priced at a forward EBITDA multiple of less than 13x; now that multiple has almost doubled to levels of 23x EBITDA (meanwhile, the industry is only priced at a 14x forward EBITDA multiple).

Prima facie, such a steep appreciation in the EBITDA multiple should’ve put me off; but also take note of the tremendous progress on the EBITDA margin front, which shows the quality you’re getting for paying such a high EBITDA multiple. As noted earlier, APP’s margins are now at a stunning threshold of 84%, whereas back in September 2024, during my previous review of the stock it was only at 57% (a sizeable 2700bps appreciation). For added context, it’s worth considering that average EBITDA margin of software sector stocks, is only around 34-36%!

Shift the valuation lens lower down the line to earnings, and what we have is a business priced at 29x forward earnings (incidentally, a 9% discount to its long-term rolling average of 32x)

And what sort of earnings growth are you going to get for shedding out a forward earnings multiple of less than 30x? Well, take a look at the image below, which showcases consensus estimates, and suggests a doubling of APP’s EPS by the end of FY27 (or 42%, on a two-year CAGR basis). 42% earnings growth at a 30x earnings multiple translates to a very enticing PEG [Price-to-earnings growth] ratio of just 0.71x

A steep EBITDA margin profile, also provides the bulwark for APP to convert a higher proportion of its sales to operating cash flow; when I last reviewed APP, its operating cash flow [OCF] margin was at 35%; now, it has almost doubled at over 68%! Then, structurally this is not a business model that has to cope with high CAPEX (which amounts to less than 2% of sales), with hosting and cloud service fees typically showing up at the COGS level (these costs too are also rather well spent as APP was one of the early adopters of Google Cloud’s G2 VMs, which saw its price to performance ratio go up by 40%; expect further improvements here as APP is now also using GOOGL’s new G4 VMs for ad serving and recommendations). A growing OCF conversion, and relatively flat CAPEX requirements, results in an operating model with a relentless cash compounding streak.

Take note of APP’s idle cash balance which is now at record highs of almost $2.5B, accounting for the largest chunk of its total asset base at 34%. When you have such a mammoth quantum of cash sitting on your balance sheet, and you don’t really have to deal with crippling CAPEX commitments, you don’t have to be parsimonious about sharing some of this cash with shareholders.

Now, while APP doesn’t pay dividends, it is certainly stepping up the pace of share buybacks, which amount to an annualized sum of $2.167B. Given this hefty share buyback spend, some investors may be wondering if APP still has enough in the tank to engage in more buybacks? Well, note that as of Dec 31st 2025, they still have a pending buyback war chest of $3.3B, which I expect will be utilized a great deal in 2026, and could help support the share price.

Technical Picture and Key Stakeholder Positioning

The image below showcases APP’s weekly price movements, and what we can see is that we first had an uptrend from March 2025 to January 2026 (with the price bouncing off an ascending trendline), which then converged to a series of lower-lows and lower-highs (from December 2025 to February 2026).

However, developments over the last three months suggest that the selling has given way to a broad flattening of the price action, with APP attempting to build a base within the $360-$520 levels. The CMP of ~450 represents a fairly ideal terrain to deploy funds for those who missed out on our initial buy call (basically there’s been a 40% retracement in the share price from its peak levels of $745.6 seen in September 2025).

Investors can also take heart from the fact that the bears appear to be losing interest in APP, with the total shares sold short declining by over 15% over the past three months.

Separately, it’s even worth noting that APP which tends to see a lot of insider sales (even during its heydays), hasn’t quite been afflicted by that for well over a month now, which also tells us that management doesn’t think this is a great time to sell, with higher prospects ahead.

Upcoming Q1 Earnings

One potential catalyst that could help APP breakout from its trading range is the company’s Q1-26 earnings event which is due to take place on the 6th of May after market closes.

It appears that consensus is quite optimistic about what APP could deliver as the revenue estimate of $1.78B is already higher than management’s guided figure of $1.76B (mid-point of guided range) provided in February. Also consider that once Q1 is out of the way, APP is set to embark on a very strong period of annual topline growth that could be within the 30-50% range for the next 10 quarters at the very least (the key trigger for this uplift could be the Self-Serve Axon Ads for e-commerce that will go live in H1-26).

What will also likely be encouraging to note in Q1 is APP’s ability to hit those 84% EBITDA margins yet again. If management can continue to guide for stabilization at these levels or further progression, it will be taken very well by the markets.

On the EPS front, expectations are for a figure of $3.64, and investors can be reasonably confident of APP beating those numbers as it has always managed to do so for 3 years now.

Risks

Last October, reports emerged that the US SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) had been investigating APP’s data-collection practices, and while one was hoping for some definitive clarity from this development - one way or the other - we are still yet to get any. In fact, as recently as February 2026, fresh reports emerged that this investigation was “still active and ongoing”. While, it’s important for investors to keep in mind that AppLovin hasn’t been accused of any wrongdoing, this lingering unresolved issue may continue to serve as an overhang on the stock’s potential to progress higher.

In the technical world, for a stock to potentially embark on sustained momentum, and also attract additional interest from momentum players, it is seen as important for it to trade above, and defend its 200DMA (Daily moving average), a key barometer of the long-term trend. While APP had managed to trade above this landmark from April 2023 all the way till February 2026, it has since fallen below its 200DMA, with a ~12% deficit currently underway.

Closing Thoughts

Despite some upheaval seen at the APP stock counter this year, I reiterate my long-standing buy rating on the stock. A superior TAM, sturdy and sustainable EBITDA margins, exceptional growth potential, cash compounding capabilities, and a higher threshold of share buybacks serve as important vectors behind my bullish stance on the APP stock.
